title: Rocket Report: India's Vikram-1 nears debut flight; AST to become rocket company?
gist: Ars Technica's weekly Rocket Report covers Skyroot Aerospace's imminent Indian orbital launch debut, China's first recovery of a reusable orbital-class booster, AST SpaceMobile's $1 billion raise to solve a launch-supply bottleneck after New Glenn's static-fire failure, a scrubbed SpaceX Starship test flight, and Ars's own cost analysis showing a proposed 1-million-satellite orbital data-center constellation would require 17,500 to 77,000 Starship launches and $1.45-9.8 trillion.

themes.theme: India's first private orbital launch attempt

insights.claim: Skyroot Aerospace's Vikram-1 rocket is set for its debut orbital launch attempt on July 18, 2026 from India, targeting a 450 km orbit at 60-degree inclination and capable of carrying up to 350 kg of small-satellite payload — the first orbital attempt by a commercially developed Indian rocket.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: A successful debut would validate India's nascent private launch sector as a new low-cost smallsat launch option, a market currently dominated by Rocket Lab and other small-launch players.
insights.locator: Vikram-1 rocket gets a launch date

themes.theme: Reusable rocket race broadens beyond SpaceX

insights.claim: China's state-owned rocket developer recovered a reusable orbital-class booster for the first time, catching a Long March 10B first stage with a four-legged, cable-tensioned frame on an offshore vessel in the South China Sea after launching from the Wenchang Commercial Space Launch Site, joining SpaceX and Blue Origin in propulsive/mechanical booster recovery.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: China closing the reusability gap threatens the launch-cost advantage that has been SpaceX's key competitive moat, with implications for global launch-market pricing and market share.

insights.claim: JAXA flew and landed its experimental RV-X reusable rocket testbed (about 7.3 meters tall, similar in form to NASA's 1990s DC-X and SpaceX's Starhopper) to roughly 11 meters altitude with a 16-meter horizontal translation over a 40-second flight, part of the Japan-France-Germany CALLISTO reusability program.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: An early but concrete step toward a non-US, non-Chinese reusable launch alternative, worth tracking as a longer-horizon entrant to the reusability race.

insights.claim: AST SpaceMobile announced a $1 billion convertible note offering explicitly to secure more launch access for its large BlueBird satellites and said it is exploring partnerships or acquisitions to vertically integrate launch capability; its stock dropped more than 10% on the announcement over dilution concerns.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: AST views third-party launch supply as a bottleneck material enough to raise $1B and consider becoming a launch operator itself — a structural risk factor for the stock and a potential M&A angle in small/medium launch.
insights.locator: AST SpaceMobile seeking other launch vehicles

insights.claim: AST's launch problem stems in part from Blue Origin's New Glenn failing a static-fire test in April 2026, which put AST's plan to launch a BlueBird satellite on New Glenn on indefinite hold.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Highlights New Glenn's reliability and cadence issues as a direct commercial risk to customers who committed to it, beyond Blue Origin's own program economics.

themes.theme: Japan's launch-cadence ambitions vs. track record

insights.claim: Japan wants to reach 30 government and commercial launches per year by the early 2030s, but has conducted only two orbital launches in all of 2026 so far (H3's return-to-flight on June 11 and Kairos's third flight, a failure, on March 4), a roughly 15x gap between ambition and current cadence that officials say may require hosting foreign rocket launches to close.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Casts doubt on near-term growth of Japan's domestic launch industry and points to a possible opening for foreign launch providers to operate from Japanese territory.
insights.locator: Japan seeks to increase launch activity

insights.claim: SpaceX's Starship Flight Test 13 was automatically scrubbed at ignition on July 16 when a handful of the booster's 33 Raptor engines failed to light; Elon Musk said the next attempt would 'hopefully' occur within a few days, and it remains unclear whether repairs can happen at the pad or require de-stacking the vehicle.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Continued Raptor ignition reliability issues at scale remain a recurring risk factor for Starship's flight cadence, relevant to Starlink V3 deployment timelines and NASA's Artemis lunar lander dependency on Starship.
insights.locator: Starship test flight is scrubbed at ignition

insights.claim: The scrubbed Starship flight was carrying 20 Starlink V3 satellites specifically to test laser inter-satellite links with SpaceX's earlier-generation Starlink fleet, a validation step for V3's interoperability rather than an operational deployment.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Successful laser-link validation is a prerequisite for SpaceX scaling its next-generation Starlink constellation and any future orbital data-relay ambitions.

themes.theme: Megaconstellation economics hinge on launch cost

insights.claim: Ars's own cost analysis of a proposed SpaceX 1-million-satellite orbital data-center megaconstellation finds cheap access to space — not radiation or cooling — is the single biggest constraint: an optimistic scenario requires 17,500 Starship launches at a total cost of $1.45 trillion, while a pessimistic scenario requires 77,000 launches (42 per day sustained over five years) at $9.8 trillion.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete order-of-magnitude cost and cadence bar for evaluating claims about space-based AI data centers — even the optimistic case implies Starship must fly roughly 10x more often than its current cadence, sustained for years.
insights.locator: How many launches to build a data center megaconstellation?

themes.theme: NASA diversifies away from ULA's Vulcan

insights.claim: NASA moved its SunRISE heliophysics mission — six small satellites forming a synthetic radio-dish array near geosynchronous orbit to study solar radio bursts — from United Launch Alliance's Vulcan rocket to a SpaceX Falcon Heavy, without announcing a new launch date.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Another data point of NASA and DoD payloads shifting from Vulcan to Falcon Heavy, consistent with a broader pattern of ULA cadence concerns benefiting SpaceX's manifest.
insights.locator: NASA mission moves to Falcon Heavy

insights.claim: A new company, Spinifex Space, launched in Queensland, Australia to provide suborbital test-range land and licensing infrastructure — static-fire testing, hypersonic vehicle testing, and destructive testing — as a private alternative to the government-owned Woomera range, spun out of solid-rocket-motor maker Black Sky Industries.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: An example of ancillary space-economy infrastructure (test ranges and licensing) emerging as its own investable niche alongside launch vehicles themselves.
insights.locator: A new suborbital test range Down Under
